subtopic_detail: HRT initiation - regimen choice in perimenopausal woman
stem: A 49-year-old woman presents to her general practice with 8 months of frequent hot flushes, drenching night sweats and irregular periods with cycles ranging from 21 to 45 days. Her last period was 6 weeks ago. She has had no abnormal bleeding between periods. Her BMI is 26, she does not smoke, blood pressure is 122/76 and she has no personal or family history of breast cancer or venous thromboembolism. She has an intact uterus and prefers reliable contraception alongside symptom relief.

data_table.Examination: No abdominal mass, no breast lump, vaginal examination unremarkable, cervical screening up to date
data_table.Investigations: FSH 18 IU/L, estradiol 200 pmol/L, TSH 1.8, fasting glucose 4.8, lipids favourable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate menopausal hormone therapy regimen?

options.text: Continuous combined oral estradiol 1 mg with medroxyprogesterone 5 mg daily started today with the simplest single daily tablet regimen for compliance benefit
options.rationale: Continuous combined regimens are designed for postmenopausal women; in perimenopause they cause unscheduled bleeding because of ongoing endogenous cycles. (Source: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy)
source_refs: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy

options.text: Estradiol only patch 50 micrograms twice weekly with no progestogen, since the patient still has endogenous progesterone production from her own ovaries during cycles
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in a woman with intact uterus causes endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma regardless of endogenous activity. (Source: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy)
source_refs: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy

options.text: Transdermal estradiol patch 50 micrograms twice weekly plus levonorgestrel intrauterine device for endometrial protection and effective contraception together
options.rationale: Transdermal oestrogen with a LNG-IUD gives effective symptom control, endometrial protection and contraception, the preferred perimenopausal combination. (Source: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy)
source_refs: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing ethinylestradiol 30 micrograms plus levonorgestrel 150 micrograms with continuation past 50 years if symptoms persist
options.rationale: Standard combined oral contraceptive doses are higher than menopausal hormone therapy and carry greater cardiovascular and VTE risk especially above 50. (Source: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy)
source_refs: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy

options.text: Sequential conjugated equine oestrogen 0.625 mg with norethisterone 1 mg for 12 days each month plus condoms for contraception, avoiding any hormonal contraceptive option
options.rationale: Oral equine oestrogen has higher VTE risk than transdermal estradiol and condoms are less reliable contraception in perimenopause. (Source: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy)
source_refs: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy

explanation.summary: Perimenopausal women still require contraception alongside symptom management. Transdermal estradiol avoids first-pass hepatic effect and has lower VTE and stroke risk than oral oestrogen, making it preferred in women over 45 or with cardiovascular risk factors. A LNG-IUD provides both contraception and endometrial protection from systemic oestrogen and is the preferred progestogen in perimenopause. Sequential or cyclical progestogen suits women still having periods to give predictable bleeds; continuous combined is for women past 12 months amenorrhoea. Stop hormonal contraception at 55 or 12 months amenorrhoea above 50.
key_points: Transdermal oestrogen has lower VTE and stroke risk than oral.
LNG-IUD covers endometrial protection and contraception.
Sequential progestogen for perimenopause, continuous when postmenopausal.
Continue contraception until 55 or 12 months amenorrhoea after 50.
Avoid HRT in active breast cancer, VTE, undiagnosed PV bleeding.
explanation.pearls: Use estradiol gel or patch and add micronised progesterone 200 mg orally at night for 12 days monthly if LNG-IUD declined - this combination has the most favourable breast cancer and VTE risk profile among oral options.

sources.label: AMS Information Sheet Menopausal Hormone Therapy
NICE NG23 Menopause Diagnosis and Management

stem: A 68-year-old postmenopausal woman attends the general practice for review after a low-trauma wrist fracture following a fall from standing height 6 weeks ago. She had a previous Colles fracture 2 years ago. Her mother had a hip fracture aged 78. She is on no current bone-affecting medications. Her DXA shows lumbar spine T-score minus 2.6 and femoral neck T-score minus 2.4. Her calcium and vitamin D levels are within range and her renal function is normal.

data_table.Examination: Healing wrist fracture, height loss 2 cm over past decade, no kyphosis, no spinal tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Corrected calcium 2.34, phosphate 1.1, 25-OH vitamin D 78, ALP 88, eGFR 78, PTH 4.2, TSH 1.6
data_table.Medications: Calcium 600 mg with vitamin D 1000 IU daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue calcium and vitamin D supplementation alone with annual DXA scanning and lifestyle advice for the next 12 months without pharmacological bone therapy
options.rationale: Pharmacological therapy is indicated for prior minimal trauma fracture or T-score below minus 2.5; calcium and vitamin D alone are inadequate. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management

options.text: Commence raloxifene 60 mg orally daily as a selective oestrogen receptor modulator for postmenopausal spinal osteoporosis with prior fragility fracture
options.rationale: Raloxifene reduces vertebral but not hip fractures and is second-line behind bisphosphonates or denosumab. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management

options.text: Initiate transdermal estradiol patch 50 micrograms twice weekly with a progestogen for endometrial protection as primary therapy for postmenopausal bone loss
options.rationale: MHT is not first-line bone therapy after 60 due to cardiovascular and breast risks though it may be considered with symptomatic women. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management

options.text: Refer for teriparatide 20 micrograms subcutaneously daily as first-line anabolic therapy for high fracture risk postmenopausal osteoporosis with a recent fragility fracture
options.rationale: Teriparatide is reserved for very high risk or PBS criteria such as a prior fracture on bisphosphonate; not first-line therapy. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management

options.text: Commence oral alendronate 70 mg weekly with calcium and vitamin D and arrange dental review before starting given the risk of medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw
options.rationale: Alendronate is first-line for postmenopausal osteoporosis with minimal trauma fracture; dental review reduces ONJ risk and ongoing calcium and vitamin D supports efficacy. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management)
source_refs: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management

explanation.summary: A minimal trauma fracture in a postmenopausal woman is an osteoporotic event regardless of DXA T-score, and is an indication for pharmacological therapy. Alendronate or risedronate are first-line oral options, with zoledronic acid annual IV for poor oral tolerance or compliance, supported by calcium 1000-1300 mg/day and vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day. Dental review and treatment of any active disease before initiation reduces ONJ risk. PBS criteria for funded therapy include T-score at or below minus 2.5, prior minimal trauma fracture, or steroid use. Reassess at 3-5 years; consider drug holiday if low risk and stable.
key_points: Minimal trauma fracture triggers pharmacological therapy regardless of T-score.
Alendronate weekly oral is first-line.
Zoledronic acid IV yearly if oral intolerance.
Pre-treatment dental review reduces ONJ risk.
Calcium 1000-1300 mg and vitamin D 800-2000 IU daily.
explanation.pearls: Denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously six-monthly is an alternative for renal impairment (eGFR below 35) but stopping abruptly causes rebound vertebral fractures - always transition to a bisphosphonate when ceasing denosumab.

sources.label: RACGP Osteoporosis Prevention Diagnosis and Management

stem: A 5-week-old first-born boy is brought to the emergency department with a 1-week history of progressively worsening non-bilious vomiting after every feed that has become projectile. He is hungry between feeds but his weight has dropped 200 g over the past week. His mother has noted reduced wet nappies. The paediatric surgeon has confirmed pyloric stenosis on ultrasound and theatre is provisionally booked for the next morning.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 168, BP 78/46, RR 36, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, sunken anterior fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 seconds, palpable olive in right epigastrium, visible gastric peristalsis after feed
data_table.Investigations: Na 132, K 2.8, Cl 88, HCO3 36, pH 7.52, BE +10, glucose 4.4, creatinine 36, ultrasound pyloric muscle thickness 5 mm length 20 mm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Place nasogastric tube on free drainage, withhold oral feeds, give intravenous 0.9 percent sodium chloride 20 mL/kg bolus then 0.9 percent saline with 20 mmol/L potassium maintenance until biochemistry corrects
options.rationale: Correction of hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with NaCl and potassium replacement is mandatory before anaesthesia; pyloric stenosis is a medical not surgical emergency. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Transfer directly to theatre for immediate Ramstedt pyloromyotomy in the next available slot, since dehydration will be most efficiently corrected by relieving the obstruction first
options.rationale: Anaesthesia with metabolic alkalosis causes hypoventilation and apnoea; biochemical correction must precede surgery. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Start a trial of small frequent feeds of thickened expressed breast milk in upright position before re-evaluating in 24 hours to see if symptoms improve with conservative measures
options.rationale: Feeding does not resolve mechanical pyloric obstruction; further vomiting worsens dehydration and electrolyte loss. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Administer intravenous ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg and observe for resolution of vomiting before deciding whether resuscitation or surgical pathway is the most appropriate option
options.rationale: Antiemetics do not treat mechanical obstruction; the diagnosis is confirmed and definitive management is fluid resuscitation then pyloromyotomy. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric Stenosis)

options.text: Commence intravenous dextrose 10 percent at maintenance rate alone without sodium chloride to address ongoing hypoglycaemia and weight loss before surgery is performed
options.rationale: Dextrose alone does not correct chloride and sodium deficits or replace gastric losses; volume and electrolyte replacement are the priorities. (Source: RCH CPG Pyloric Stenosis)

explanation.summary: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis classically presents at 3-6 weeks in first-born males with projectile non-bilious vomiting, hunger between feeds, weight loss and a palpable olive. Ultrasound confirms with pyloric muscle thickness at least 3 mm and length at least 15 mm. Vomiting of gastric HCl causes hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria; this must be corrected before anaesthesia to prevent post-operative apnoea. Resuscitation uses 0.9 percent saline boluses then maintenance with added potassium once urine output established. Ramstedt pyloromyotomy is curative; feeding is reintroduced within hours postoperatively.
key_points: Projectile non-bilious vomiting in 3-6 week first-born male.
Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis is classic.
Correct biochemistry before anaesthesia to prevent apnoea.
Aim chloride above 100 and bicarbonate below 30 before theatre.
Ramstedt pyloromyotomy is curative; feed within hours.
explanation.pearls: Add potassium only after urine output is established (above 1 mL/kg/h) and never give as a stat bolus; standard maintenance fluid is 0.9 percent saline with 20-40 mmol/L KCl, never glucose-water alone in alkalosis.

sources.label: Australian Paediatric Surgery Guidelines - Pyloric Stenosis

stem: A 56-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his partner with 3 days of progressive headache, confusion, fever and one witnessed generalised seizure today. He has no significant medical history and is up to date with his vaccinations. He is drowsy with GCS 13, disoriented, has slow word-finding speech and no focal neurology. There is no rash and no neck stiffness on examination. He has had no recent travel or sick contacts.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6, HR 102, BP 138/86, RR 18, SpO2 97 percent room air
data_table.Examination: GCS 13 (E3V4M6), disoriented, no meningism, no focal weakness, no rash, no papilloedema
data_table.Investigations: WCC 10, CRP 32, Na 132, glucose 6.4, creatinine 82, ECG sinus rhythm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous aciclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours empirically before any imaging or lumbar puncture is performed for suspected herpes simplex viral encephalitis
options.rationale: Empirical aciclovir within 6 hours significantly reduces mortality and morbidity in HSV encephalitis; never delay for imaging or LP. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Encephalitis)

options.text: Perform urgent lumbar puncture for CSF analysis and viral PCR before any therapy is commenced to confirm the diagnosis and identify the responsible pathogen accurately
options.rationale: Empirical aciclovir should not be delayed for LP because diagnostic confirmation takes hours and delay increases morbidity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Encephalitis)

options.text: Order urgent non-contrast CT brain and EEG, then commence ceftriaxone 4 g daily plus dexamethasone for bacterial meningitis cover pending further results from investigations
options.rationale: Bacterial meningitis cover alone misses HSV encephalitis; aciclovir must be added immediately to any empirical regimen. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Encephalitis)

options.text: Administer intravenous levetiracetam 1500 mg loading dose for seizure control and observe with neurological observation until brain imaging and CSF results return for review
options.rationale: Anticonvulsants treat seizure activity but do not address the underlying encephalitis; aciclovir is the time-critical intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Encephalitis)

options.text: Treat with intravenous foscarnet 60 mg/kg every 12 hours as first-line antiviral therapy for adult viral encephalitis given the higher potency over standard aciclovir options
options.rationale: Foscarnet is reserved for aciclovir-resistant HSV in immunocompromised patients; aciclovir is first-line empirical therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Encephalitis)

explanation.summary: HSV-1 encephalitis is the most common sporadic viral encephalitis with mortality above 70 percent untreated, falling to 20-30 percent with prompt aciclovir. Classic features are subacute fever, headache, behavioural change, focal seizures and temporal lobe signs, with MRI brain showing asymmetric temporal lobe T2 hyperintensity. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, raised protein, normal glucose; HSV PCR is highly sensitive after 48 hours of symptoms. Empirical aciclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours IV starts before imaging or LP and continues for 14-21 days. Add bacterial meningitis cover (ceftriaxone plus dexamethasone) if meningism present pending results.
key_points: Empirical aciclovir IV before imaging or LP if encephalitis suspected.
Aciclovir 10 mg/kg q8h for 14-21 days.
MRI temporal lobe T2 hyperintensity is classic.
CSF HSV PCR may be negative in first 48 hours - repeat if clinical suspicion.
Add bacterial cover if any meningism or rash.
explanation.pearls: Maintain renal function with prehydration and dose-adjust aciclovir for renal impairment - crystalline nephropathy is a recognised reversible toxicity, monitor creatinine daily during therapy.

subtopic_detail: Asherman syndrome - investigation of secondary amenorrhoea
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman attends the gynaecology clinic with 8 months of secondary amenorrhoea following a uterine evacuation for retained products of conception 10 months ago after a missed miscarriage at 11 weeks. She has not been using contraception. She had previously regular cycles. She denies hot flushes, weight changes, headaches, galactorrhoea or hirsutism. She is keen for another pregnancy. Her general health is otherwise unremarkable.

data_table.Examination: No goitre, no galactorrhoea, no hirsutism, normal external genitalia, bimanual unremarkable, no acanthosis
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative, FSH 6 IU/L, LH 5 IU/L, estradiol 280 pmol/L, prolactin 280 mIU/L, TSH 1.6, AMH 12 pmol/L

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: MRI pituitary to exclude a microadenoma as the underlying cause of her secondary amenorrhoea despite a normal serum prolactin level on screening tests done today
options.rationale: Pituitary imaging is unnecessary when prolactin is normal and the clinical setting points to a structural endometrial cause. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

options.text: Day 21 progesterone level to confirm ovulatory cycles after which she should be referred for tubal patency testing if amenorrhoea persists beyond 3 further months
options.rationale: Day 21 progesterone is unreliable in amenorrhoea (no day 21 reference) and does not address the most likely intrauterine adhesion diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

options.text: Empirical 12-week course of cyclical oral oestrogen 2 mg with sequential progestogen with reassessment after 3 months for resumption of menses before further investigation
options.rationale: Hormonal therapy without investigating intrauterine pathology delays diagnosis; structural adhesions will not respond to hormone therapy alone. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

options.text: Saline infusion sonohysterography or hysteroscopy as the investigation of choice for suspected intrauterine adhesions following uterine instrumentation in pregnancy loss
options.rationale: Direct visualisation by saline sonohysterography or hysteroscopy is the standard investigation for suspected Asherman syndrome after instrumentation. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy with chromopertubation to assess for adhesion formation involving the fallopian tubes and ovaries following the previous miscarriage event 10 months ago
options.rationale: Laparoscopy evaluates pelvic structures but not the uterine cavity which is the site of suspected pathology here. (Source: RANZCOG Asherman Syndrome)

explanation.summary: Asherman syndrome (intrauterine adhesions) classically follows uterine instrumentation, especially repeated evacuation of retained products of conception, and presents as secondary amenorrhoea or hypomenorrhoea with infertility. The hormonal axis is intact: FSH, LH, oestradiol, prolactin, TSH and AMH are normal. Diagnosis is by saline infusion sonohysterography or hysteroscopy. Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis is the treatment, with adjuncts (oestrogen, balloon, intrauterine devices) to prevent re-adhesion and improved pregnancy outcomes after treatment. Future evacuations should be done under ultrasound guidance to minimise recurrence.
key_points: Asherman = intrauterine adhesions after instrumentation in pregnancy.
Normal hormonal axis with secondary amenorrhoea after D and C.
Diagnose with saline sonohysterography or hysteroscopy.
Treat with hysteroscopic adhesiolysis and adhesion prevention.
Future evacuations under ultrasound guidance.
explanation.pearls: When recurrent miscarriage prompts repeat evacuation, suction curettage under ultrasound guidance halves the risk of new adhesion formation compared with blind sharp curettage; counsel and plan for image-guided procedures.

sources.label: AAGL Practice Guidelines on Intrauterine Adhesions

subtopic_detail: Adult bacterial meningitis - empiric therapy with dexamethasone
stem: A 32-year-old previously well woman presents with 18 hours of severe headache, fever, neck stiffness, photophobia and one episode of vomiting. She is alert. Examination demonstrates positive Brudzinski sign and significant neck stiffness. She has no rash. She has no allergies and is not pregnant. The CT brain has been arranged but is delayed and a lumbar puncture has not yet been done. She is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1, HR 108, BP 128/76, RR 18, SpO2 98 percent room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, severe neck stiffness, positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs, no rash, no focal neurology, GCS 15
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18, neutrophils 16, CRP 240, lactate 1.6, Na 138, glucose 7.2, coags normal, blood cultures sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate empirical therapy?

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g every 12 hours without adjunctive corticosteroid because steroids have inconclusive benefit in adult community-acquired bacterial meningitis
options.rationale: Adjunctive dexamethasone improves outcomes (reduced hearing loss and mortality) in pneumococcal meningitis and is given empirically in Australian guidelines. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Meningitis)

options.text: Defer all antibiotic therapy until non-contrast CT brain excludes raised intracranial pressure followed by diagnostic lumbar puncture for CSF analysis and culture
options.rationale: Antibiotics must not be delayed for imaging or LP in suspected meningitis; first-dose within 1 hour is the primary goal. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Meningitis)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily with paracetamol as outpatient management for community-acquired meningitis in a well-appearing patient without focal neurology
options.rationale: Suspected bacterial meningitis requires intravenous antibiotics and inpatient management; oral therapy is inadequate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Meningitis)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose monotherapy and proceed directly to lumbar puncture for CSF testing without empirical beta-lactam coverage first today
options.rationale: Vancomycin monotherapy does not adequately cover meningococcus or Listeria; combination with a third-generation cephalosporin is needed in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Meningitis)

options.text: Intravenous dexamethasone 10 mg immediately followed by ceftriaxone 2 g every 12 hours with benzylpenicillin 1.8 g every 4 hours pending CSF results
options.rationale: Empirical regimen for adult bacterial meningitis is ceftriaxone plus benzylpenicillin with dexamethasone given just before or with the first dose of antibiotic. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Meningitis)

explanation.summary: Adult bacterial meningitis requires immediate empirical IV antibiotics within 1 hour of suspicion. The Australian standard regimen is ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h plus benzylpenicillin 1.8 g IV q4h for pneumococcus, meningococcus and Listeria cover, with adjunctive dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h for 4 days given just before or with first antibiotic dose to improve outcomes especially in pneumococcal disease. Add vancomycin if penicillin-resistant pneumococcus is a concern, ampicillin in immunocompromised or above 50 for Listeria. CT before LP if focal neurology, seizures, GCS below 14, immunocompromise or papilloedema. Notify public health for meningococcal disease.
key_points: First-dose IV antibiotic within 1 hour of suspicion.
Ceftriaxone plus benzylpenicillin empirically in Australia.
Dexamethasone with or just before first dose - reduces hearing loss and mortality.
CT before LP only if focal signs, seizures, GCS below 14, immunocompromise.
Notify public health and give meningococcal prophylaxis to close contacts.
explanation.pearls: Add ampicillin 2 g q4h to cover Listeria in patients over 50, pregnant women, or immunocompromised - cephalosporins have no activity against Listeria and missing this cover increases mortality.

subtopic_detail: Vulval lichen sclerosus - diagnosis and treatment
stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with 12 months of progressive vulval itch, soreness and recurrent splitting at the introitus during intercourse. She has been using over-the-counter clotrimazole intermittently without lasting benefit. Examination reveals pale porcelain-white plaques over the labia minora and clitoral hood with loss of architecture, fusion of the prepuce over the clitoris and a small fissure at the posterior fourchette. There is no ulceration or palpable nodule.

data_table.Examination: Pale porcelain-white labial plaques, fused clitoral hood, narrowed introitus, posterior fourchette fissure, no ulceration, no palpable mass, no inguinal lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Vulval swab no growth, urinalysis unremarkable
data_table.Medications: Nil topical apart from clotrimazole previously

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue topical clotrimazole 1 percent twice daily for 4 weeks before further specialist review since chronic vulvovaginal candidiasis remains the most likely diagnosis here
options.rationale: Architectural change and porcelain-white plaques are not consistent with chronic candidiasis; lichen sclerosus is the likely diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Lichen Sclerosus)

options.text: Initial vulval biopsy of representative pale plaque area followed by ultra-potent topical clomethasone propionate ointment 0.05 percent nightly for 4 weeks with planned taper
options.rationale: Biopsy confirms diagnosis and excludes differential vulval intraepithelial neoplasia; ultra-potent topical steroid is the first-line treatment for lichen sclerosus. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Lichen Sclerosus)

options.text: Wide local surgical excision of the affected vulval tissue with primary closure as first-line treatment for vulval lichen sclerosus given the visible architectural change and fusion
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for biopsy-confirmed neoplastic change or severe scarring causing functional problems unresponsive to medical therapy. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Lichen Sclerosus)

options.text: Vaginal oestrogen pessary 10 micrograms estradiol twice weekly for 6 weeks as primary therapy for atrophic vulvovaginitis given postmenopausal age and dyspareunia symptoms
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen may help atrophy but does not treat lichen sclerosus, which requires steroid therapy after biopsy. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Lichen Sclerosus)

options.text: Oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose for chronic vulvovaginal candidiasis with empirical management and review in 4 weeks if symptoms persist after a single weekly dose course
options.rationale: Oral antifungal is not indicated when there is no microbiological evidence of candidiasis and morphology suggests lichen sclerosus. (Source: RANZCOG Vulval Lichen Sclerosus)

explanation.summary: Vulval lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis causing porcelain-white atrophic plaques, architectural distortion (loss of labia minora, clitoral phimosis, introital narrowing), itch and dyspareunia. Diagnosis is clinical with biopsy to confirm and exclude differential VIN or squamous cell carcinoma. First-line treatment is ultra-potent topical steroid (clobetasol propionate 0.05 percent ointment) nightly for 4 weeks, then alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance. Lifelong follow-up because of about 5 percent risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma. Daily emollient, avoid soap, treat any concurrent candidiasis or dermatitis.
key_points: Porcelain-white plaques with architectural distortion - think lichen sclerosus.
Biopsy confirms diagnosis and excludes VIN or SCC.
Clobetasol 0.05 percent ointment is first-line.
Lifelong monitoring - 5 percent risk of vulval SCC.
Use emollient, avoid soap, treat candida or dermatitis.
explanation.pearls: Resistant lichen sclerosus or steroid-tachyphylaxis can be managed with topical tacrolimus 0.1 percent off-label, but use under specialist supervision because the long-term risk of SCC with calcineurin inhibitors on vulval skin is not fully established.

sources.label: British Association of Dermatologists Lichen Sclerosus Guideline

subtopic_detail: Induction of labour - choice based on Bishop score
stem: A 32-year-old G1 woman at 41+3 weeks gestation attends the day assessment unit for induction of labour following post-dates pregnancy. She has been counselled regarding the increased risks beyond 41 weeks and consented. Antenatal care has been uneventful with a normal CTG and biophysical profile today. Membranes are intact. Her booking BMI was 24 and the baby is in a cephalic presentation on the scan today.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72, HR 78, T 36.6, CTG reactive with normal baseline 135
data_table.Examination: Cervix 1 cm dilated, 30 percent effaced, soft, mid position, vertex at -2 station (Bishop score 4)
data_table.Investigations: Group B Streptococcus screen negative at 36 weeks, ultrasound estimated fetal weight 3.6 kg, normal liquor

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step for induction of labour?

options.text: Intravenous oxytocin titrated infusion starting at 1 milli-unit per minute and doubling every 30 minutes with continuous CTG monitoring without prior cervical ripening method first
options.rationale: Oxytocin is inefficient on an unripe cervix (Bishop 4) and significantly increases caesarean rate; pre-ripening is required first. (Source: RANZCOG Induction of Labour)

options.text: Artificial rupture of membranes alone with planned reassessment in 4 hours and oxytocin commencement at that time if regular contractions have not yet been established
options.rationale: Amniotomy alone with unfavourable cervix often fails; cervical ripening before ARM improves successful vaginal delivery rates. (Source: RANZCOG Induction of Labour)

options.text: Membrane sweep at clinic visit then a further week of expectant management with twice-weekly CTG monitoring before reassessing the need for active induction of labour
options.rationale: Beyond 41+0 weeks, induction is recommended; further expectant management increases stillbirth and perinatal morbidity risk. (Source: RANZCOG Induction of Labour)

options.text: Misoprostol 200 micrograms orally with planned repeat doses 4 hourly without continuous monitoring required given the previous uneventful antenatal course and the cervix score
options.rationale: Misoprostol requires CTG monitoring during ripening due to risk of uterine tachysystole and fetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG Induction of Labour)

options.text: Cervical ripening with prostaglandin E2 vaginal gel or balloon catheter, repeat Bishop assessment in 6 hours and progression to artificial rupture of membranes plus oxytocin when favourable
options.rationale: Bishop score under 6 requires cervical ripening with prostaglandin or balloon first, followed by ARM and oxytocin once the cervix is favourable. (Source: RANZCOG Induction of Labour)

explanation.summary: Bishop score combines cervical dilation, effacement, consistency, position and station to determine induction approach. Score below 6 indicates unripe cervix and need for cervical ripening (prostaglandin E2 gel or pessary, or mechanical balloon catheter) before amniotomy and oxytocin, while Bishop at least 7 (favourable) allows direct ARM with oxytocin. Continuous CTG monitoring is needed with prostaglandins due to risk of hyperstimulation. Induction at 41+0 to 41+3 reduces perinatal mortality versus expectant management. Always counsel on indication, alternatives, success rates and risk of failed induction.
key_points: Bishop score guides induction method.
Below 6 - ripen with prostaglandin or balloon first.
At least 7 - direct ARM plus oxytocin.
Continuous CTG with prostaglandin induction.
Induce at 41+0 to 41+3 weeks to reduce stillbirth.
explanation.pearls: Mechanical methods (Cook double balloon or single Foley) are preferred over prostaglandins for ripening in women with prior caesarean, asthma or fetal heart rate concerns; balloon does not cause tachysystole and avoids the hyperstimulation risk of prostaglandins.

stem: A 44-year-old man attends the practice asking for help to quit smoking. He has smoked 20-25 cigarettes daily for 25 years and is highly nicotine dependent (Fagerstrom score 7). He has tried short-acting nicotine replacement gum once before unsuccessfully. He has no significant mental health history and is not on any psychiatric medications. He has well-controlled type 2 diabetes and asthma. His renal function is normal and he is keen to set a quit date for 2 weeks from now.

data_table.Examination: Chest clear, no cyanosis, no oral changes
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 6.8 percent, fasting glucose 6.4, eGFR 92, urinalysis unremarkable, PHQ-9 score 3 (none-minimal)

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological smoking cessation therapy?

options.text: Combination of long-acting nicotine patch 21 mg per 24 hours together with short-acting nicotine inhaler used in response to cravings for the next 12 weeks as outpatient
options.rationale: Combination NRT is a valid choice but varenicline has higher quit rates and remains first-line in highly dependent smokers without contraindication. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Bupropion sustained release 150 mg daily for 3 days then 150 mg twice daily for 7 to 12 weeks starting today, with quit date set in the first 2 weeks of therapy as planned
options.rationale: Bupropion is effective but second-line; it lowers seizure threshold and varenicline has higher quit rates as first-line therapy. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks with a quit date set in week 2 of treatment
options.rationale: Varenicline titrated over week 1 with quit date in week 2 is first-line in nicotine-dependent smokers without contraindication and outperforms NRT and bupropion. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Brief intervention with motivational interviewing alone without pharmacotherapy and arranging a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks to discuss further options if no progress made
options.rationale: Brief intervention without pharmacotherapy in a highly nicotine-dependent smoker underuses effective treatment and lowers quit success rates. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

options.text: Clonidine 0.1 mg twice daily as off-label smoking cessation pharmacotherapy with planned monitoring of blood pressure during the first 4 weeks of titration therapy
options.rationale: Clonidine is third-line because of sedation and rebound hypertension and is rarely used in Australia compared with first and second-line options. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation)

explanation.summary: Combined behavioural and pharmacological therapy doubles quit rates. Pharmacological first-line options in Australia for nicotine-dependent smokers are varenicline, combination NRT (patch plus short-acting), or NRT alone. Varenicline (alpha-4 beta-2 nicotinic receptor partial agonist) has the highest quit rates; titrate from 0.5 mg daily over week 1 to 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks, with quit date in week 2. Monitor for nausea (most common), sleep disturbance, vivid dreams; warnings on neuropsychiatric symptoms have been downgraded after EAGLES trial showed no significant increase. Bupropion is second-line; avoid in seizure or eating disorder history.
key_points: Combine behavioural and pharmacological for best quit rates.
Varenicline first-line in nicotine-dependent smokers.
Titrate varenicline over week 1 to 1 mg BD; quit date in week 2.
Combination NRT (patch plus short-acting) is equally effective alternative.
Bupropion second-line; avoid with seizure or eating disorder.
explanation.pearls: Varenicline can interact with insulin needs in diabetes - improved cessation often improves glycaemic control quickly so monitor home BSL closely in the first 4 weeks and titrate hypoglycaemic medications down as needed.

subtopic_detail: Cystic fibrosis newborn screening - positive result followup
stem: A 3-week-old well-appearing baby is brought in by her parents after they receive a phone call from the newborn screening program saying the test was positive for cystic fibrosis. The baby was term, weighed 3.4 kg at birth, has been gaining weight well on breastfeeding and is passing 6 wet nappies per day and stools 3 times per day. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis. The parents are anxious and want to understand what happens next.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 142, RR 38, SpO2 99 percent room air
data_table.Examination: Well, alert, normal tone, chest clear, abdomen soft, weight tracking 25th centile, no clubbing
data_table.Investigations: Newborn screening IRT 240 ng/mL (above 99th centile cut-off), CFTR gene panel pending, no symptoms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure the family and arrange repeat blood spot newborn screening in 4 weeks if the baby remains well, given the positive screening test result on the initial sample today
options.rationale: Positive IRT plus pending CFTR genotyping requires definitive diagnostic confirmation, not repeat screening. (Source: RCH CPG Cystic Fibrosis)

options.text: Commence empirical pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy at 2000 lipase units per kg per feed with weekly clinic monitoring while diagnostic confirmation is pending
options.rationale: Treatment is not started until diagnosis is confirmed by sweat chloride; empirical therapy is inappropriate. (Source: RCH CPG Cystic Fibrosis)

options.text: Arrange an immediate plain chest x-ray to look for early lung disease and barium meal to exclude meconium ileus given the positive newborn screening test result
options.rationale: Imaging is not first-line in asymptomatic CF screen-positive infants; diagnostic confirmation by sweat chloride is required. (Source: RCH CPG Cystic Fibrosis)

options.text: Refer urgently to the local paediatric cystic fibrosis centre for diagnostic sweat chloride test and full CFTR mutation analysis with multidisciplinary team review
options.rationale: Sweat chloride is the gold standard diagnostic test for CF after positive newborn screening; CF centre referral coordinates testing, counselling and surveillance. (Source: RCH CPG Cystic Fibrosis)

options.text: Send a faecal elastase sample and stool fat estimation now to evaluate for pancreatic insufficiency before confirming the cystic fibrosis diagnosis with further specialist tests
options.rationale: Faecal elastase assesses pancreatic function but does not diagnose CF; sweat chloride is the diagnostic standard. (Source: RCH CPG Cystic Fibrosis)

explanation.summary: Australian newborn screening uses immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT) on day 2-3 dried blood spot, with elevated IRT (above 99th centile) prompting CFTR mutation panel. A positive screen requires confirmatory sweat chloride at a recognised CF centre by Gibson-Cooke method; chloride at least 60 mmol/L is diagnostic, 30-59 is intermediate (extended CFTR sequencing and clinical correlation needed), under 30 is normal. Early diagnosis and multidisciplinary care (paediatrician, dietitian, physiotherapist, psychologist) improves growth, lung function and survival. Pancreatic enzyme replacement, fat-soluble vitamins, airway clearance and CFTR modulators (in eligible mutations) are core treatments.
key_points: Newborn screening: IRT day 2-3 then CFTR panel if elevated.
Sweat chloride at CF centre is the definitive diagnostic test.
Chloride above 60 mmol/L diagnostic, 30-59 intermediate, under 30 normal.
CF centre coordinates multidisciplinary follow-up.
CFTR modulators (ivacaftor and combinations) transformed prognosis.
explanation.pearls: Suspect CF clinically even with a normal screen if meconium ileus, recurrent chest infections or failure to thrive develops - 1 in 20 CF cases are missed by screening due to rare mutations not on the panel.

sources.label: Australasian Cystic Fibrosis Standards of Care
